lesson_title: Const class objects and const member functions

cards.text: A {{c1::const}} class object cannot have its data members modified after initialization.
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
class Date { public: int year{}; };
const Date today{2024};
today.year = 2025;
cards.answer: Cannot modify a member of a const object. today.year is const and cannot be assigned to.
cards.explanation: Const class objects have all their data members treated as const, preventing modification.
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.text:
To define a const member function (that doesn't modify the object):
ReturnType functionName() {{c1::const}}
{
    // implementation
}
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.front: What member functions can be called on const class objects?
cards.back: Only const member functions can be called on const class objects. Non-const member functions cannot be called.
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.explanation: Const member functions guarantee they won't modify the object. This allows them to be called on const objects and communicates design intent. It's a best practice to make all member functions that don't modify the object const.
examples: void print() const { std::cout << m_value; }  // doesn't modify
void setValue(int x) { m_value = x; }  // modifies, not const
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.text: Inside a const member function, the implicit object is treated as {{c1::const}}, preventing modification of any data members.
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: A const member function cannot modify data members. ++count modifies count, which is not allowed.
cards.explanation: Const member functions promise not to modify the object. Attempting to modify count violates this promise and causes a compiler error.
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.text: When defining a const member function outside the class, the {{c1::const}} keyword must appear after the parameter list in both the {{c2::declaration}} and {{c3::definition}}.
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.front: What is const-correctness in the context of classes?
cards.back: Const-correctness means properly using const for class objects and member functions that don't modify the object. All non-modifying member functions should be marked const.
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.question: Which member function can be called on the const object?
cards.code:
class String
{
public:
    int length() const;
    void clear();
};

const String str{"hello"};
// Which works: str.length() or str.clear()?
cards.answer: str.length() works because it's const. str.clear() doesn't work because it's non-const.
cards.explanation: Const objects can only call const member functions. length() is const so it's allowed, but clear() would modify the object so it's not allowed.
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.back: A member function that promises not to modify the object, marked with const after the parameter list
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions

cards.front: What is the best practice for passing class objects to functions by const reference?
cards.back: Pass class objects by const reference (const ClassName&) to avoid expensive copies. This requires the class to have proper const member functions for any operations you want to perform.
cards.source: 14.4 - Const class objects and const member functions
